architecture_summary: data2vec is a general self-supervised learning framework that uses the same masked-prediction learning objective across speech, vision, and language. It predicts contextualized latent representations of the full input from a masked view using a standard Transformer in teacher/student mode with an EMA teacher and layer-averaged targets.

novel_contributions: A general self-supervised learning framework that uses the same learning method for speech, NLP and computer vision.
Predicts contextualized latent representations of the full input rather than modality-specific discrete targets such as words, visual tokens or speech units.
Uses the average of multiple teacher network layers as training targets and shows this is more effective than predicting only the top layer.
Shows that contextualized latent targets and the same learning objective work across images, speech and text.
Reports a new state of the art for ViT-B and ViT-L on ImageNet-1K, improved speech recognition results, and better GLUE performance than a RoBERTa baseline.

key_claims.claim: data2vec uses the same learning method for speech, NLP and computer vision by predicting latent representations of the full input from a masked view in a self-distillation setup.
The method predicts contextualized latent representations instead of modality-specific targets such as words, visual tokens or speech units.
Experiments on speech recognition, image classification, and natural language understanding show a new state of the art or competitive performance to predominant approaches.
data2vec outperforms prior work with ViT-B and ViT-L in the single-model ImageNet-1K setting, and all prior work for ViT-L.
For speech, data2vec improves over wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT on low-resource Libri-light setups, with the largest gains at 10 minutes of labeled data.
For GLUE, data2vec outperforms the RoBERTa baseline and improves further with wav2vec 2.0 style masking of spans of four BPE tokens.
Averaging multiple teacher layers is more robust than predicting only the top layer, and using all layers is generally a good choice.
Contextualized target representations improve accuracy, with the best performance when the entire input sample is visible to the teacher.
